risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Following the initial assessment, each child was randomly assigned to one of the four treatment conditions." Comment: The paper explicitly states that allocation was random, and the overall description is consistent with a randomized controlled trial. Although it does not specify the exact randomization mechanism, the wording is sufficient under a permissive standard to judge sequence generation as low risk, with no evidence of a non-random or predictable allocation rule.

Quote: "Following the initial assessment, each child was randomly assigned to one of the four treatment conditions." Comment: The report states that participants were randomly assigned and that the project coordinator was blind to treatment assignment, but it does not describe any mechanism that would prevent the recruiter from foreseeing the next allocation (e.g., central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, or identical containers). Therefore allocation concealment cannot be judged as low risk and remains unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Because the project coordinator was blind to treatment assignment, participants were told of the treatment assignment by the therapist during the first treatment session." Comment: Participants and therapists were not blinded to allocation; indeed participants were explicitly informed of their assignment by the therapist. Because the intervention was psychotherapy and the outcomes included subjective child/parent reports and behavioral ratings (e.g., anxiety, fear, parenting practices, externalizing behavior), knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence outcomes across the study overall, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "the project coordinator was blind to treatment assignment" and "Randomly selected tapes from different stages of treatment were also reviewed by independent raters who were blind to the assigned treatment type". Comment: Outcome assessment was mainly by patient/parent self-report for several outcomes, and by an independently administered K-SADS interview; the coordinator was blinded and independent raters were blinded. Because the key outcomes include clinician/interviewer-rated PTSD symptoms from an independent assessment and many other outcomes are self-report/parent-report questionnaires, the explicit blinding of the project coordinator and independent raters supports low risk for detection bias for the assessed outcomes, and the objective/structured nature of the interview-based measure further reduces the likelihood that knowledge of allocation influenced measurement.

Quote: "As Figure 1 displays, 16 (8%) children and their parents never returned after being assigned to treatment, and 15 (7%) children and their parents left after attending only one or two sessions. These 31 (15%) children and their parents were defined as dropouts... A MM-ANCOVA is robust with respect to the occurrence of randomly missing data. Consequently, all of the available data at each evaluation were employed in the mixed model analyses... A modified intent-to-treat approach was also used to assess the impact of missing data upon ANCOVA results... All of the parameter estimates from the MM-ANCOVAS with the multiple imputation datasets were similar to those that had been found without imputing missing data." Comment: Attrition was moderate (15% dropouts), reasons were simply early treatment discontinuation rather than clearly outcome-related, and the analysis used mixed models plus multiple imputation with consistent results. This supports a low risk judgement for attrition bias.
